description: Unified three-layer RAG retrieval ablation: PyMuPDF (baseline) -> MinerU (no_gate) -> MinerU + GLM online arbitration (gated). One source PDF, one QA set, one relevance test across all three corpora, so Hit@k is comparable layer to layer. The PDF (mineru_paper.pdf) embeds scanned image pages of an accelerometer energy-expenditure study: PyMuPDF reads only the digital text layer (the MinerU paper itself) and cannot reach the image-page content at all; MinerU OCR reads the images but joins words together (missing spaces); DocGate's GLM visual arbitration re-inserts the word boundaries. Relevance is WORD-BOUNDARY preserving: text is lowercased, HTML/LaTeX stripped, non-alphanumerics collapsed to single spaces (spaces KEPT). A chunk is relevant if its normalized text contains any answer phrase. Because spaces are kept, a run-on passage no longer spuriously matches a spaced answer phrase, so the second hop (MinerU -> +GLM) is visible. NOTE: this relevance test differs from the deprecated MinerU-vs-PyMuPDF set (which stripped spaces); the two are intentionally not comparable.

queries.query: What were the study purposes regarding energy expenditure prediction from accelerometer data and the performance of three different accelerometers?
answer_norm: three different accelerometers on five different wear positions

queries.query: How many children participated in the study and what percentage were boys?
Which prediction models were compared, including the mixed linear model and random forest?
On how many different wear positions were the accelerometers placed on the preschoolers?
What cross-validation scheme was used to evaluate prediction accuracy?
What semi-structured protocol did the children complete during data collection?
What was concluded about the random forest as a viable alternative to linear and ANN models?
answer_norm: rf seems to be a viable

queries.query: Why do highly structured laboratory protocols tend to overestimate energy expenditure in children?
answer_norm: limits the transfer to free living
